text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 3 October 2015 

Dr Anita Shah 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
Kingston Hospital NHS Foundation Trust  

Dear Dr Anita Shah, 

Alex Morgan, DOB: 22-08-1992, Hospital No: K847210 NHS No. 3014729986  
Flat 12, 9 Riverbank Way, Kingston upon Thames, KT1 2AB  

Diagnosis: 

Generalised epilepsy with recent clusters of brief seizures; valproate responsive but with residual breakthrough episodes

Aetiology: 

Presumed genetic generalised epilepsy; no structural lesion identified; normal development and no perinatal complications

Other Medical Problems: 

Generalised anxiety and low mood, patient reports intermittent sleep disturbance and health-related worry; no self-harm; currently engaged with GP for talking therapy referral

Management Plan: 

- Continue sodium valproate modified release 500 mg mane and 1000 mg nocte.  
- Introduce rescue plan for clusters: buccal midazolam 5 mg if three or more brief seizures occur within 30 minutes, or if a seizure lasts longer than 5 minutes; mother instructed and provided written guidance.  
- Sleep hygiene advice and stress-management strategies discussed; encourage ongoing counselling via IAPT.  
- Safety advice: avoid sleep deprivation, limit alcohol, and adhere to DVLA guidance for seizures.  
- Arrange ambulatory EEG and serum valproate level prior to next visit; routine bloods including FBC, LFTs.  
- Review in four months, earlier if further clusters occur.

This patient has generalised epilepsy and describes variable brief events without consistent focal onset features. In April he had a run of 6 seizures within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In June there was six further seizures at night, and in September another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother). He did not require emergency care and there has been no associated injury. He links the periods of increased seizure activity to poor sleep and heightened anxiety. Valproate has otherwise reduced background events, and there have been no prolonged generalised tonic–clonic seizures for over a year. We discussed a pragmatic rescue plan for clusters, reinforcement of adherence, and strategies to address anxiety and sleep, as these appear to act as triggers. No adverse effects from valproate reported; weight stable and mood symptoms predate treatment. I will update you following the EEG and blood results.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: In April he had a run of 6 seizures within half an hour... In June there was six further seizures at night, and in September another during physiotherapy

In April he had a run of 6 seizures within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In June there was six further seizures at night, and in September another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother)

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter describes discrete seizure clusters/events on three occasions within the recent months: a run of 6 seizures in April (within 30 minutes), six further seizures in June (at night), and another seizure in September. There is no explicit ongoing regular frequency given beyond these dated episodes. Over the 6-month window surrounding these described months (e.g., April to September inclusive is roughly 6 months), the total number of seizures explicitly mentioned is 6 (April) + 6 (June) + 1 (September) = 13. There is no statement of being seizure-free for ≥6 months. Therefore, infer the frequency as 13 per 6 months. Quote provided from the letter references the counts.
seizure_frequency_number: In April he had a run of 6 seizures within half an hour... In June there was six further seizures at night, and in September another during physiotherapy
reference: In April he had a run of 6 seizures within half an hour (not full generalised tonic-clonic, fluctuating in intensity, resolved without medication). In June there was six further seizures at night, and in September another during physiotherapy (unwitnessed by mother)
